summary: hpsetup CLI fetches mutable tarballs from non-publisher.xyz CDN, exfiltrates license keys, and persists itself into Vercel build pipelines
details: When invoked, `hpsetup` downloads tarballs from `https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz/api/tarball/<slug>/<version>?key=<userKey>` (src/constants.js:16, src/download.js:23) and extracts them into `node_modules/@heroui-pro/react` and `heroui-native-pro`, which the consumer's application then `require()`s as production code. The destination is a random-numeric `.xyz` host with no apparent relationship to HeroUI's publisher domain (heroui.com); the tarballs are unsigned and not hash-verified, so whoever controls `932324.xyz` can serve arbitrary JavaScript that runs inside every consumer application. The user's paid license key is appended as a query string parameter on every fetch, relaying it to that same third-party host. The CLI also writes `@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk` — an unrelated OS-keychain access SDK — into pnpm `onlyBuiltDependencies` / `pnpm-workspace.yaml` allowBuilds / Bun `trustedDependencies` (src/trust.js:5), silently granting that package's postinstall scripts unattended execution privileges on consumer machines. Finally, `configureVercel` (src/vercel.js:18-35) writes `installCommand: "<dlx> hpsetup@latest <userKey>"` into `vercel.json` and appends `vercel.json` to `.gitignore`, causing every future Vercel deploy to re-fetch the mutable `hpsetup@latest` and pass the license key in argv — a change hidden from code review. The combination of mutable-CDN code delivery from a non-publisher domain, third-party allowlist injection, and gitignored auto-update persistence gives whoever controls 932324.xyz a durable channel to ship arbitrary code into every consumer's application and CI.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
